source.title: A selective role for dopamine in stimulus-reward learning

confidence_rationale: Causally and neurochemically isolates the computation MECH-292's 'wanting' multiplier abstracts: a dopamine-gated transformation of a predictive cue into an action-invigorating incentive. High source quality (voltammetry + DA antagonism + selective-line behaviour, Nature). Held below 0.85 because the biology says incentive attribution is a separable, individually-variable gated process -- not a scalar that automatically scales with predictive match, which the bank's multiplicative term implicitly assumes.
failure_signatures: In sign-trackers a cue becomes attractive and 'wanted' even when approaching/contacting it does not improve outcome -- wanting detaches from instrumental value (a craving/compulsive-attention signature).
Incentive salience persists and drives behaviour when the predicted reward's value should not warrant pursuit, conferring impulse-control vulnerability.
Goal-trackers show the inverse failure: a fully predictive cue never gains motivational pull -- a 'blocked goal never re-probed' signature.

mapping.source_claim_statement: A reward-predictive cue can be used either as a pure predictor (goal-tracking) or have incentive salience attributed to it so the cue itself becomes 'wanted' (sign-tracking); phasic dopamine transfers to cue onset only in sign-trackers, and dopamine antagonism selectively blocks acquisition of the incentive (sign-tracking) response while sparing the predictive (goal-tracking) response.
mapping.ree_translation: Provides the mechanistic anchor for MECH-292's 'wanting' multiplier and for MECH-293 re-probing being motivationally driven: matching a cue to a stored trace is not sufficient to invigorate pursuit -- a dopamine-gated step converts a matched predictive trace into an action-driving incentive. The 'wanting' term in ghost_priority is naming a real, separable computation.
mapping.mapping_caveat: Incentive attribution here is a trait-variable, dopamine-gated process that some individuals barely express even for fully predictive cues. An artificial 'wanting x goal_match' product assumes every well-matched cue receives motivational gain; the biology says gain is a distinct gated process dissociable from predictive accuracy, so the multiplicative coupling is a simplification the data does not endorse.
mapping.source_context: Selectively bred sign-tracker vs goal-tracker rats; Pavlovian lever-CS autoshaping; fast-scan cyclic voltammetry in nucleus accumbens core; systemic flupenthixol.

confidence_components.notes: Source quality very high (causal + neurochemical + selective-line, Nature). Mapping fidelity moderate: it validates the existence and separability of a 'wanting' computation but argues against its being a simple multiplicative function of match. Transfer risk moderate (rodent Pavlovian conditioning -> artificial goal bank).
